key_learnings: Iron, vitamin D, B12, folate, iodine, and zinc are the most prevalent global micronutrient deficiencies. Deficiency symptoms are frequently non-specific and overlapping, making dietary pattern analysis essential for early detection. Bioavailability varies widely depending on food matrix, preparation method, and co-consumed nutrients.
research_findings: Vitamin D deficiency affects over 40% of adults globally and is associated with immune dysfunction, bone loss, and mood disorders. Iron deficiency anemia is the leading nutritional disorder worldwide, disproportionately affecting women and children. Phytate and oxalate compounds in plant foods significantly inhibit zinc and calcium absorption without proper food preparation techniques.
current_focus_areas: Building a symptom-to-deficiency mapping algorithm; identifying high-risk dietary patterns predictive of specific micronutrient deficiencies; developing food-first correction strategies prior to supplementation recommendations; coordinating department-wide findings with co-department members Bot 1, Bot 2, and Bot 20.
cross_learning_directive: Directed all co-department members — Bot 1 (Nutrigenomics), Bot 2 (Microbiome Analysis), and Bot 20 (Supplement Recommendations) — to independently study every sibling domain within the Nutritional Assessment & Genomics department and share findings across the team.

co_department_learning_progress.cross_learning_status: Independently studying Microbiome Analysis and Supplement Recommendations domains; sharing genomic insights with department.
Independently studying Nutrigenomics and Supplement Recommendations domains; sharing microbiome data with department.
Independently studying Nutrigenomics and Microbiome Analysis domains; aligning supplement evidence with department deficiency models.
